judgments.rationale: DC revenue growth continues to outrun shipment capacity even as the slope flattens.
judgments.mechanism: Backlog and purchase commitments keep growing faster than shipment volumes, the signature of demand still pulling ahead of supply.
judgments.falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
judgments.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex guidance over the next two quarters.

judgments.rationale: Rising Intel channel inventory days show the chain is still packaging/memory-bound, not demand-bound.
judgments.mechanism: CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity and HBM memory supply, not wafer starts or end demand, keep setting the ceiling on finished-GPU shipments this quarter.
judgments.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM lead times normalize to historical norms while demand indicators stay positive for 2 quarters.
judgments.sensitivity: Sensitive to announced advanced-packaging and HBM capacity additions from packaging and memory vendors.

judgments.rationale: AMD's MI-series and custom-ASIC erosion of NVIDIA share keeps tracking toward ~75%, consistent with a credible second source emerging.
judgments.mechanism: AMD's Instinct MI-series roadmap and hyperscaler design-win cadence continue to determine whether it becomes a credible, at-scale second source in datacenter accelerators.
judgments.falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters without a named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale.
judgments.sensitivity: Sensitive to named hyperscaler deployment announcements and AMD's execution against its MI-series shipment cadence.

judgments.rationale: Hyperscalers internalizing silicon toward the ~75% accelerator-share mark keeps custom ASICs on track to displace merchant-GPU demand growth.
judgments.mechanism: Hyperscalers' in-house training and inference accelerator programs keep competing directly with merchant-GPU purchases for the same capex dollars.
judgments.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler capex mix shifts back toward merchant GPUs, or a named ASIC program is cancelled/delayed a generation, within 2 quarters.
judgments.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex-mix disclosures and the shipment/deployment cadence of named custom-silicon programs.

judgments.rationale: CUDA lock-in remaining the dominant switching barrier keeps rental/spot pricing power intact despite added supply.
judgments.mechanism: Sustained demand for GPU compute, reinforced by CUDA switching costs, keeps rental and spot prices firm even as new capacity comes online.
judgments.falsifiableTrigger: The D6 price track shows a decline of 15% or more across 2 or more providers within a quarter.
judgments.sensitivity: Sensitive to the D6 rental price track across providers and the pace at which new GPU capacity enters the rental market.

judgments.rationale: No new licensing rollback or reversal this cycle; export-control exposure remains a live constraint on addressable demand.
judgments.mechanism: Export-control restrictions on advanced GPUs continue to limit the addressable market in restricted destinations, redirecting or destroying demand that would otherwise be served.
judgments.falsifiableTrigger: A full licensing regime rollback, or 2 consecutive quarters with no new policy action and no vendor-reported impact.
judgments.sensitivity: Sensitive to new export-control rulemaking, licensing decisions, and vendor-reported revenue-impact disclosures.
